description: Management establishes reporting lines, authority, responsibility, and oversight relationships for internal control and key operational decisions.

subcriteria.name: reporting_structure_and_role_definition
subcriteria.reference_text: An organizational chart, team structure document, or role-definition artifact shows reporting lines and core responsibilities for executives and key control functions. Real evidence often includes who reports to whom, key responsibilities for security, compliance, engineering, or legal, and annual review or acknowledgment of role definitions.

subcriteria.name: authority_and_approval_responsibilities
subcriteria.reference_text: A governance document or responsibility matrix shows which roles approve, certify, or oversee internal control activities. Real evidence often says access administrators approve specific tasks, managers certify reviews, executives sponsor control programs, or committees receive recurring control reporting.

subcriteria.name: segregation_and_independent_responsibilities
subcriteria.reference_text: A strong governance artifact shows that incompatible duties are separated across roles. Real evidence often says developers cannot approve their own pull requests, deployers are separate from approvers, access administrators cannot review their own accounts, finance approvals require dual approval, or incident responders are distinct from investigators.

subcriteria.name: external_party_and_oversight_roles
subcriteria.reference_text: The document may also define who handles external-party interactions or board oversight related to control responsibilities. Real evidence often mentions vendor-management roles, customer security commitments, trust-center ownership, independent director oversight, or quarterly committee reporting.

mismatch_signals.reason: Security tooling evidence is not primarily organizational authority and reporting-line evidence.
Resilience infrastructure evidence is not primarily governance structure evidence.
